title: Confidence interval versus p-value
intro: The p-value answers "is the difference chance?", while the confidence interval answers "how big is it and how sure are we?". They are two sides of one coin: estimation and testing. Understand their link — and you stop worshipping "p < 0.05" and start reading results like a grown-up analyst.

beats.text: There is an exact connection between them. The chart shows the effect estimate with its confidence interval relative to zero. Drag the effect and the sample size: at exactly the moment the interval stops covering zero, p drops below 0.05. "p < 0.05" and "the interval excludes zero" are the same statement said two ways.
But the interval says MORE than the p-value.
beats.predict: Two tests both gave p = 0.04. The first has an effect interval of [0.1%, 0.3%], the second [0.1%, 40%]. Are these equally useful results?
beats.reveal: No! Both are "significant", but the first says "the effect is small and precisely measured", the second "the effect could be tiny or huge, we know almost nothing". The p-value hides that; the interval shows both magnitude and precision. That is why reporting the interval is more honest.

beats.text: The interval cures two diseases of the p-value. First: "significant" ≠ "important". A narrow interval around a negligible effect is statistically significant yet useless for the business; the interval shows it instantly. Second: "not significant" ≠ "no effect". A wide interval covering zero says "data is scarce", not "there is no difference".

Hence modern practice (and many journals' requirements): report not a bare p but the ESTIMATE WITH ITS INTERVAL: "B lifted conversion by 1.8% (95% CI: 0.3%–3.3%)". You immediately see: the effect is significant (zero not covered), its plausible size, and the uncertainty. That is thinking in terms of estimation rather than the ritual of significance.

The link to the course's backbone: both the p-value and the confidence interval grow out of one object — the estimate's sampling distribution. The p-value measures where zero sits on it; the interval traces its plausible width around the estimate. Not two methods but two views of one picture.

definitions.text: estimation answers "how much and how precisely" (the interval); testing answers "is it chance" (the p-value). Linked through one sampling distribution.
a 95% interval excludes zero ⟺ the two-sided test is significant at 5%.
a statistically significant effect can be negligible in size; look at the interval / effect size.
a wide interval around zero means insufficient data, not proven absence of an effect.

decision: If the decision depends on "how much to invest", decide by the interval, not by "significant/not": the interval shows both the best and the worst plausible outcome.
practice: The habit of reporting results as intervals, not just p, immediately raises decision quality: you see direction, size and confidence. "A 1.8% lift (CI 0.3–3.3%)" is grounds for a decision; "p = 0.04" is not.

It also dissolves the cult of 0.05: instead of "significant / not significant" the team discusses what effect is plausible and whether it is worth shipping. Especially vital on large samples, where almost everything becomes significant.
realLife: Medicine long ago switched to confidence intervals for effect sizes: "a 20% risk reduction (95% CI: 5–33%)" is more informative than "p < 0.05".

In a product, the interval around an A/B effect feeds the rollout decision directly: if even the interval's lower bound pays for the costs — ship; if the interval is wide and touches zero — collect more data.
